nativeRange.habitat: Shallow rocky reefs and sand-weed areas at roughly 1–4 m depth; typically observed sheltering inside vacant mollusc tubes, vermetid snail tubes, and old barnacle shells on rocky outcrops along the eastern Pacific coast.

diet.notes: Has been reported to feed on small benthic invertebrates and zooplankton in the wild. In captivity it tends to accept finely chopped meaty preparations, vitamin-enriched frozen brine shrimp, frozen mysis shrimp, and similar small-particle frozen foods. Best fed small portions multiple times per day so the reclusive fish gets a chance to dart out for food.

summary: The Eyebrow Barnacle Blenny (Ekemblemaria myersi) is a small tube-dwelling blenny from shallow rocky reefs along the eastern Pacific coast. It spends most of its time tucked inside vacant mollusc or barnacle shells, typically exposing only its head, and is generally considered reef-safe.
careNotes:
The Eyebrow Barnacle Blenny (Ekemblemaria myersi), also known as the Reefsand Blenny or Myers' Blenny, is a small tube blenny in the family Chaenopsidae native to the eastern central Pacific, ranging from the Gulf of California south through Mexico, Costa Rica, Panama, and Colombia. In the wild it inhabits shallow rocky outcrops in sand-weed habitat at roughly 1–4 m depth, where it occupies vacated mollusc tubes, vermetid snail tubes (the coiled, worm-like tubes built by a type of sessile marine snail), and old barnacle shells. Like other tube blennies, it typically rests with only its head protruding from the opening.

It is generally regarded as reef-safe and well suited to mature reef tanks with abundant fine rockwork and rubble. The species tends to be highly reclusive, spending most of the day partially concealed inside a tube or crevice and darting out briefly to seize passing food. A well-established system furnished with many small holes, vermetid tubes, and barnacle shells will give it the shelter it needs to settle and feed confidently. Sand-bed and rubble zones alongside the rockwork are commonly recommended.

Pairing this blenny with boisterous, fast-feeding tankmates is generally cautioned against: food can easily be intercepted before this small, hesitant feeder leaves cover. Feeding small portions multiple times per day helps ensure it gets adequate nutrition. Adults reach around 5–7 cm in length.

Like many small blennies, this species tends to be a jumper; a tightly fitting lid is strongly advised. Its coloration is dark brown to black with 10–12 darker bars and a prominent ocellated spot on the gill cover (the bony plate that protects the gills, also called the operculum).

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Source of husbandry parameters: pH (8.1–8.4), SG (1.020–1.025), temperature 72–78°F, dKH 8–12, semi-aggressive temperament, reef-safe, carnivore diet, 30 gal minimum (~114 L), max ~2.75 in (~7 cm), reclusive behavior. Note: page lists family as 'Antennariidae', which is incorrect (Chaenopsidae is the accepted family per FishBase and Wikipedia).

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: fishbase.org returned HTTP 403; the .se mirror was used. Cross-checked taxonomy (Chaenopsidae, Blenniiformes), maximum length (7.0 cm TL), distribution (Gulf of California to Colombia), depth range (1–4 m), habitat (mollusc tubes on rocky outcrops in sand-weed areas), preferred temperature (23.3–29.1°C, mean 27.4°C), diet (invertebrates), trophic level 3.5, IUCN Least Concern.

additional.name: Wikipedia (Ekemblemaria myersi)
additional.notes: Confirmed family Chaenopsidae, order Blenniiformes, common name 'Reefsand blenny', range Gulf of California to Colombia, max length 7 cm, feeds on zoobenthos, described 1963 by J. S. Stephens, named after George S. Myers. Used for taxonomy fallback after LiveAquaria misclassified the family.
